fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to get the tokenized research project registry service shipped. basically the idea is researchers can register their IP-backed projects and get a full workspace spun up automatically — like accounts, a place to store files with versioning, and a feed for updates/announcements. all through one API surface so devs don't have to wire everything together themselves.

the tricky parts we keep forgetting to spec out properly: when two teams try to register the same project (or reuse a symbol someone else already claimed), we need to handle that gracefully — not just blow up. also the enable/disable flow from that lifecycle module we built before should behave the same way here (you know, the idempotency stuff and the different error messages depending on whether the project ever existed vs just currently inactive).

for files, people need to upload with all the metadata — the encryption stuff, categories, tags, access levels, etc — and get proper versioning automatically. announcements need to validate that any attached files actually exist before accepting the post.

the code needs to be properly structured, not one giant file. separate the API layer, domain logic, storage abstractions. think about what happens when someone queries a project that's been disabled vs one that never existed — those should feel different to the caller.

check how we handled the conflict resolution and not-found vs no-history distinction in the existing login/account module for reference.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: createProject(ipnftSymbol: String!, ipnftUid: String!, ipnftAddress: String!, ipnftTokenId: Int!): CreateProjectResult

trigger_keywords: how does the account name get derived from the symbol
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The ipnftSymbol is always normalized to lowercase both for storage and for the account name derivation. For example, submitting 'VITAFAST' results in ipnftSymbol stored as 'vitafast' and an account name of 'molecule.vitafast'. The account name format is always 'molecule.' + lowercased symbol.

trigger_keywords: what happens when same uid is registered twice
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a createProject mutation is attempted with an already-registered ipnftUid (or already-used symbol), the response is NOT a top-level GraphQL error. Instead it returns a typed result inside data with __typename 'CreateProjectErrorConflict', isSuccess: false, and a message of the form: 'Conflict with existing project <lowercased-symbol> (<ipnftUid>)'. The catalog is left unchanged.

trigger_keywords: difference between disable and enable error for unknown uid
project not found vs no history
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: disableProject on an unknown uid returns a top-level GraphQL error 'Project <uid> not found' with null data. enableProject on a uid that has never existed returns a top-level GraphQL error 'No historical entries for project <uid>' with null data. These two error messages are intentionally different: disable uses 'not found', enable uses 'no historical entries'.

Disabling is NOT idempotent. Calling disableProject a second time on an already-disabled project returns a top-level GraphQL error 'Project <uid> not found' with null data, because a disabled project is no longer visible/findable. Enabling, by contrast, IS idempotent — enabling an already-enabled project succeeds.

trigger_keywords: can I reuse a symbol from a disabled project
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A symbol remains reserved even while its project is disabled. Attempting to create a new project with the same symbol as a disabled project returns a CreateProjectErrorConflict result (not a top-level error), naming the original project in the message. This prevents symbol squatting across the disable/enable lifecycle.

The contentHash is computed deterministically from the file's decoded bytes (the Base64-encoded content is decoded first, then hashed). The same binary content will always produce the same contentHash. This hash is stored per version and returned in the uploadFile response under entry.asVersionedFile.latest.contentHash.

trigger_keywords: what version is the first upload
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: File versioning starts at version 1 for the first upload of any given path. Each subsequent upload to the same path increments the version number by 1. The latest version object carries this integer version field.

trigger_keywords: what happens if attachment ref does not exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When publishing an announcement, any attachment refs are validated against existing data room entries before the announcement is accepted. If an attachment ref does not correspond to a known file in the project's data room, the mutation returns an error-typed result (not a top-level GraphQL error) indicating the attachment is invalid. The announcement is not persisted.

trigger_keywords: what does the id look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Generated account ids and dataset ids follow a 'did:odf:...' format. Because these are non-deterministic across runs, the test adapter normalizes them to stable placeholders: the first encountered did:odf value becomes <did-0>, the second <did-1>, and so on. UUIDs are similarly normalized to <uuid-0>, <uuid-1>, etc. The same raw value always maps to the same placeholder within a single test case.

The data room alias is always '<accountName>/data-room' and the announcements alias is always '<accountName>/announcements', where accountName is 'molecule.' + lowercased symbol. For example, symbol 'VITAFAST' yields data room alias 'molecule.vitafast/data-room' and announcements alias 'molecule.vitafast/announcements'.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be structured
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The implementation must NOT be a single file. It must be organized into at minimum: a GraphQL/API surface layer, core domain use-case modules (provisioning, lifecycle, data room, announcements), and versioned-storage abstractions. The stdin/stdout adapter is separate from the core domain. This mirrors a production-grade repository structure per the architectural constraints.

trigger_keywords: when is the project catalog created
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the test case input carries 'create_projects_dataset: true', the empty project catalog dataset is provisioned before any operations run. If that flag is omitted, the catalog is auto-created on the first createProject mutation call. Querying projects before any createProject on a pre-created catalog returns an empty nodes array.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: test cases for feature1 project provisioning
show me feature1 project provisioning test cases
what are the feature1 project provisioning test inputs
give me the feature1_project_provisioning test file
what does feature1 project provisioning expect
test cases for feature2 project lifecycle
show me feature2 project lifecycle test cases
what are the feature2 project lifecycle test inputs
give me the feature2_project_lifecycle test file
what does feature2 project lifecycle expect
test cases for feature3 data room
show me feature3 data room test cases
what are the feature3 data room test inputs
give me the feature3_data_room test file
what does feature3 data room expect
show me feature4 announcements test cases
what are the feature4 announcements test inputs
give me the feature4_announcements test file

context_pointers.vague_reference: the idempotency stuff and the different error messages depending on whether the project ever existed vs just currently inactive — check the lifecycle module we built before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2 (Project Lifecycle) in start.md and feature2_project_lifecycle.json: disableProject returns 'Project <uid> not found' (top-level error, null data) for unknown uids; enableProject returns 'No historical entries for project <uid>' (top-level error, null data) for uids that never existed; enableProject is idempotent but disableProject is not.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check how we handled the conflict resolution and not-found vs no-history distinction in the existing login/account module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The conflict resolution pattern is implemented in the createProject mutation returning CreateProjectErrorConflict typed result (not a top-level error), while not-found conditions in disableProject/enableProject return top-level GraphQL errors with null data — both patterns are specified in feature1_project_provisioning.json and feature2_project_lifecycle.json.

The MoleculeEncryptionMetadataInput block with fields: dataToEncryptHash, accessControlConditions, encryptedBy, encryptedAt, chain, litSdkVersion, litNetwork, templateName, contractVersion — all stored and echoed verbatim in the uploadFile response under entry.asVersionedFile.latest.encryptionMetadata.

Feature 4 (Announcements): an append-only announcements stream per project, accessible via the announcements dataset alias '<accountName>/announcements', with attachment validation against existing data room entry refs before acceptance.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That aspect isn't explicitly covered in the current requirements. Please refer to the existing test case contracts in the public_test_cases directory and the PRD in start.md for the closest analogous behavior, and implement the most conservative/defensive interpretation that keeps the typed error model consistent with the patterns already established for other features.

feature1_project_provisioning.json.description: Provision an IP-NFT-identified project and list it in the catalog; a second project reusing the same unique id is rejected as a conflict.

feature2_project_lifecycle.json.description: Disabling or enabling an unknown project surfaces a not-found / no-history error.

cases.expected_output:
--- operation 1 ---
errors:
  - Project foo not found
data:
null
--- operation 2 ---
errors:
  - No historical entries for project foo
data:
null

feature3_data_room.json.description: Upload a versioned file into a project's data room and read it back from the entry listing.

feature4_announcements.json.description: Publish an announcement with a valid attachment and list it; an announcement referencing a non-existent attachment is rejected.
